m_Item1.QuestionText:
public abstract class Shape {
      private int x;
      private int y;
      public abstract void draw();
      public void setAnchor(int x, int y) {
          this.x = x;
          this.y = y;
      }
}
Which of the following declared class use the Shape class correctly?

Options.OptionText:
public class Circle extends Shape {
private int radius;
public void draw();
}

public abstract class Circle implements Shape {
private int radius;
public void draw();
}

public class Circle extends Shape {
private int radius;
public void draw() {/* code here */}
}

public abstract class Circle implements Shape {
private int radius;
public void draw() {/* code here */}
}

m_Item1.Explanation: Shape class is abstract here so we have to inherit the class. It is a responsibility of subclass. In option C Class circle extends Shape and we implement abstract draw() method which is correct implementation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which one of the following is not true about abstract class?

Options.OptionText: A class containing abstract methods is called an abstract class.
Abstract methods should be implemented in the derived class.
An abstract class cannot have non-abstract methods.
A class must be qualified as ‘abstract’ class, if it contains one abstract method.

m_Item1.Explanation: <font color="#0000ff">An abstract class can have non-abstract(concrete) methods.</font>

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following statements about abstract methods/classes in Java is/are true?
i) An abstract class cannot be instantiated.
ii) Constructors cannot be abstract.
iii) A subclass of an abstract class must implement the abstract methods otherwise atleast declare itself as abstract.
iv) Static methods may be declared abstract.
m_Item1.Explanation:
Rules for Java Abstract Class

•We can declare an abstract class using the abstract keyword.
•We can have abstract and non-abstract methods in an abstract class.
•An abstract class can have constructors and static methods.
•An abstract class can have final methods which will force the subclass to not change the body of the method.
•We cant instantiate an abstract class.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given:
interface Jumper {
public void jump();
}
class Animal { } 
class Dog extends Animal {
Tail tail;
}
class Beagle extends Dog implements Jumper {
public void jump() {}
}
class Cat implements Jumper {
 public void jump() {}
}
Which of the following is true?
m_Item1.Explanation: Dog has tail and Beagle extends Dog. So Beagle has-a relationship with Tail.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following class definitions define a legal abstract class?

Options.OptionText: public class abstract A{abstract void unfinished();}

m_Item1.Explanation: When the method in the class is declared as abstract, class should be compulsorily declared as abstract. Abstract keyword should be written before class keyword.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given:
public class Base
{
public static final String FOO = "foo";
public static void main(String[] args)
{
Base b = new Base();
Sub s = new Sub();
System.out.print(Base.FOO);
System.out.print(Sub.FOO);
System.out.print(b.FOO);
System.out.print(s.FOO);
System.out.print(((Base)s).FOO);
}
}
class Sub extends Base
{
public static final String FOO="bar";
}
What is the result?
m_Item1.Explanation: Static members can be accesible by class name as well as objects. So Base class FOO varibale has a value "foo" and Sub class FOO variable has a value "bar". In the last statement object s is type casted to Base, so it prints FOO variable from Base class. So outcome is "foobarfoobarfoo".

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which interface out of the following is not a marker interface
m_Item1.Explanation:
• Marker interface is that having not methods declared(no abstract methods) but it can have static and default methods implemented
  • It is an empty interface (no field or methods).
  • Examples of marker interface are Serializable, Clonnable and Remote interface. All these interfaces are empty interfaces.
  •        public interface Serializable       {
  •        // nothing here        }

m_Item1.QuestionText:
1)final    2)abstract   3)private
i)can't access outside the class
ii)can’t override
iii)can’t instantiate
Select correct pair:
m_Item1.Explanation:
1. Final method can not be overridden.
  2. Abstract class can not  be instantiated.
  3. Private data members can not be accessed outside the class

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given:
public abstract interface Frobnicate
{
public void twiddle(String s);
}
Which is a correct class?

Options.OptionText:
public abstract class Frob implements Frobnicate {
public abstract void twiddle(String s) { }
}
public abstract class Frob implements Frobnicate { }

public class Frob extends Frobnicate {
public void twiddle(Integer i) { }
}

public class Frob implements Frobnicate {
public void twiddle(Integer i) { }
}

m_Item1.Explanation: Frobnicate needs to be implemented as it is interface. The implementing class should provide concrete implementation of abstract method twiddle(String s). If not atleast implementing class should be declared as abstract. So option B is correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given:
interface TestA{
String toString();
}
public class Test{
    public static void main(String[] args) {
             System.out.println(new TestA(){
public String toString()
{
return "test";
}
});
}
}
What is the result?

Options.OptionText: An exception is thrown at runtime.

m_Item1.Explanation: Interface instance is created by using anonymus instance and required toString() method is overridden which displays "test".

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement from the following is true?

Options.OptionText: local variable can be declared as final variables
An abstract class can be declared as final
A class can inherit constructors from any of it's super classes
Two methods may have the same name and argument list but different return types

m_Item1.Explanation:
The java final keyword can be used in various context:
◾final variable - A variable declared as final prevents the content of that variable being modified
◾final method - A method declared as final prevents the user from overriding that method
◾final class - A class declared as final cannot be extended thus prevents inheritance

Example:

final class TestClass
{
//this class can't be subclassed/extended
  final double pie=3.14;//this variable can only be initialized once
  private final Integer testMethod(final String input){ //this method can't be overidden
    return 0;
  }
}

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following are modifiers that can be applied to a method declaration within an interface?
i. abstract
ii. final
iii. private
iv. protected
v. public
m_Item1.Explanation: Interface can have methods declared as public and abstract

m_Item1.QuestionText:
interface A 
{
  final void m1();     // 1
  synchronized void m2(); // 2
  native void m3();     // 3
  abstract void m4();    // 4 
  public void m5();     // 5
}
Compile-time errors are generated at which lines?
m_Item1.Explanation:
An interface in java is a blueprint of a class. It has static constants and abstract methods. The interface in Java is a mechanism to achieve abstraction.
Syntax:
1.interface <interface_name>{  
2.      
3.    // declare constant fields  
4.    // declare methods that abstract   
5.    // by default.  
6.}

m_Item1.QuestionText: To prevent any method from overriding, we declare the method as,
m_Item1.Explanation: Final methods can not be overridden,

m_Item1.QuestionText: The fields in an interface are implicitly specified as,
m_Item1.Explanation: Interface fields are by default treated as static and final.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will happen when you attempt to compile and run the following code
class Base {
protected int i = 99;
}
public class Ab {
private int i=1; 
public static void main(String argv[])
{
Ab a = new Ab();
a.hallow();
}
abstract void hallow()
{
System.out.println("Claines "+i);
}
}

Options.OptionText: Compilation and not output at runtime

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract methods should not have body defined. So it results in compilation error.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
interface TestA { String toString(); }
public class Test {
public static void main(String[] args) {
System.out.println(new TestA() {
public String toString() { return "test"; }
});
}
}
What is the result?

Options.OptionText: An exception is thrown at runtime.
Compilation fails because of an error in line 1.

m_Item1.Explanation: In given example, within TestA() constructor is defined then toString() method is called which return "test".

m_Item1.QuestionText:
public  abstract  interface  Manager  
{
   public void interview(String s);
}
Which  is  a  correct class definition out of following?

Options.OptionText:
public void abstract  class HiringManager    implements   Manager
{
        public  abstract  void interview  (String s)    {}
}
public abstract   class   HiringManager    implements   Manager {}

public class  HiringManager   extends  Manager
{
   public   void interview(lnteger   i)  {}
}

public class  HiringManager   implements  Manager  
{
   public void interview  (Integer    i) {}
}

m_Item1.Explanation: Class should implement Manager interface and override the method public void interview(String s)  or declare itself as abstract.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A class which cannot have a  subclass  is called as ___________  class
m_Item1.Explanation: When a class is declared as final, is can not be inherited further i.e. it's subclass can not be created.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What are marker interfaces in Java?

Options.OptionText: The interfaces with no method declaration
The interface with no method implementation.

m_Item1.Explanation:
• Marker interface is that having not methods declared(no abstract methods) but it can have static and default methods implemented.
  • It is an empty interface (no field or methods).
  • Examples of marker interface are Serializable, Clonnable and Remote interface. All these interfaces are empty interfaces.

        public interface Serializable
        {  
        // nothing here
        }

m_Item1.QuestionText: Name any two marker interfaces in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Serializable and cloneable are empty interface in java.
  • Examples of marker interface are Serializable, Clonnable and Remote interface. All these interfaces are empty interfaces.

        public interface Serializable
        {  
        // nothing here
        }

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given:
abstract  interface   Pixie  {
abstract   void  sprinkle();
static  int  dust=    3;
}
abstract  class  TinkerBell  implements   Pixie {
String  fly() {
return    "flying";
}
}
public  class  ForReal  extends   TinkerBell {
public   static  void  main(String[]    args) {
new   ForReal().sprinkle();
}
public   void sprinkle()  { 
System.out.println(fly()  +"   "+   dust);  
}
}
What  is the  result?

Options.OptionText: Compilation fails because TinkerBell doesn't properly implement Pixie
Compilation fails because ForReal doesn't properly extend TinkerBell.
Compilation fails because Pixie is not a legal interface

m_Item1.Explanation: Program compiles fine and gives the outcome as "flying 3". Using abstract modifier for interface is redundant but will not generate any error . It will give warning.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Keyword used to inherit  Interface from  Interface
m_Item1.Explanation:
One interface can extends another interface.
​

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statement is not true?

Options.OptionText: An interface that is declared within the body of a class or interface is known as a nested interface.
A final variable cannot be a member of an interface.
A class declaration can be a member of an interface.
If an interface is named in the implements clause of a class, then the class must implement all of the methods declared within the interface.

m_Item1.Explanation: Final variable can be a member of interface. So it is not true.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider the following code in file Sample.java
public class Sample implements IInt {
public static void main(String[] args)  {
Sample s = new Sample(); //1
int j = s.thevalue; //2
int k = IInt.thevalue; //3
int l = thevalue; //4
}
}
interface IInt {
int thevalue = 0;
}
What will happen when the above code is compiled and run?

Options.OptionText: It will give an error at compile time at line //1
It will give an error at compile time at line //2
It will give an error at compile time at line //3
It will compile and run without any problem.

m_Item1.Explanation: Interface variables can be accessed in the implementing class by using instance variable or by using interface name or directly.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which is the valid declarations within an interface definition?
m_Item1.Explanation: Interface methods can not be final and protected. Method can be declared as static in interface but then it should have method body.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a valid method declaration ?
m_Item1.Explanation:
• Method has to have return type, abstract methods can not have method body.
  • Abstract methods are those which need to be implemented in subclass/child class.
  • Abstract methods are only defined in superclass/parent class(Abstract class) but with no body.
  • Syntax of Abstract Method-->
  • abstract class animal
       {
               abstract void eat(); // abstract method
               void talk(){
               System.out.println(Welcome in Know-It");} //concrete method
    }
  • A method which is not abstract i.e. if a methods definition is given in the same class its declared is called concrete.     
​​​​​​​

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Choose Invalid declaration of “k” from below options for :
public interface Foo { /* Line 3 */}
m_Item1.Explanation: Variable can not be declared as abstract

m_Item1.QuestionText:
public abstract class Shape {
      private int x;
      private int y;
      public abstract void draw();
      public void setAnchor(int x, int y) {
          this.x = x;
          this.y = y;
      }
}
Which of the following classes use the Shape class correctly?

Options.OptionText:
public class Circle implements Shape {
private int radius;
}

public class Circle extends Shape {
private int radius;
public void draw();
}

public abstract class Circle implements Shape {
private int radius;
public void draw();
}

public class Circle extends Shape {
private int radius;
public void draw() {/* code here */}
}

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract class needs to be extended not implemented. When we extend the class by abstract class then  either declare the class as abstract or give the implementation of abstract method.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
1.  public interface A {
2.           public void doSomething(String thing);
3.  }
1.  public class AImpl  implements  A {
2.            public void doSomething(String msg) {}
3.  }
1.  public class B {
2.          public A doit(){
3.                //more code here
4.          }
5.          public String execute(){
6.                 //more code here
7.          }
8.  }
1.  public class C extends B {
2.          public AImpl doit(){
3.                  //more code here
4.          }
​​​​​​​5.
6.           public Object execute() {
7.                   //more code here
8.           }
9.  }

Options.OptionText: Compilation will succeed for all classes and interfaces.
Compilation of class C will fail because of an error in line 2
Compilation of class C will fail because of an error in line 6
Compilation of class AImpl will fail because of an error in line 2.

m_Item1.Explanation: In overridden method return should be same or as by co-varient return type rule. Subclass overridden method should return the same type or subtype as that of return type in supeclass method.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statement is false?

Options.OptionText: Variables declared inside of interface declaration may be changed by implementing class
Interfaces are designed to support dynamic method resolution at run time
When an interface method is implemented it must be declared as public

m_Item1.Explanation: Data member inside the interface is public and  final and hence can not be changed by implementing classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
class Main
{
public static void main(String args[])
{
final int i;
i = 20;
System.out.println(i);
}
}
m_Item1.Explanation: It prints value 20. Final variables can be declared in method without immediate initialization.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which  is a valid keyword in java?
m_Item1.Explanation:
• interface is a valid keyword which is used to declare interfaces. unsigned is not a keyword. 
  • String and Float are the names of classes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following class definitions defines a legal abstract class?
m_Item1.Explanation: Sequence of keywords should be abstract class for class declaration. Class has to be declared as abstract when the any method in the class is  declared as abstract.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given:
interface Foo {}
class Alpha implements Foo {}
class Beta extends Alpha {}
class Delta extends Beta {
public static void main( String[] args ) {
Beta x = new Beta();
//insert code here
}
}
Which code, inserted at line 16, will cause a java.lang.ClassCastException?
m_Item1.Explanation:
x is of type Beta.
Beta is not a subclass of Delta; rather, Delta is a subclass of Beta.
Therefore, attempting to cast a Beta object (x) to a Delta type will result in a ClassCastException because Beta is not a Delta.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
import java.util.*;
class GFC107 {
public static void main (String args[])
{
Object a = new HashSet();
Object b = new HashMap();
Object c = new Hashtable();
System.out.print((a instanceof Cloneable)+",");
System.out.print((b instanceof Cloneable)+",");
System.out.print(c instanceof Cloneable);
}
}
What is the result of attempting to compile and run the program?
m_Item1.Explanation: HashSet, HashMap as well as Hashtable have implemented Cloneable interface.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Suppose that an interface, I1, is not a member of an enclosing class or interface. Which of the following modifiers can not be applied to interface I1?
m_Item1.Explanation: Interface which is not declared as nested interface can be only declared as public and abstract. Only inner interfaces can be declared as static.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will be output of following output?
interface I10 {String name = "I10"; String s10 = "I10.s10";}
interface I20 {String name = "I20"; String s20 = "I20.s20";}
class C20 implements I10, I20{     
public static void main(String[] args){
System.out.print(I10.s10+",");   
System.out.print(I20.s20+",");   
System.out.print(I20.name);    
}
}
m_Item1.Explanation: E

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following are modifiers that can be applied to a field declaration within an interface?
m_Item1.Explanation: Within the interface data members can be declared only by using the keywords - public, static and final

m_Item1.QuestionText:
class Leg { }
class Fur { }
abstract class Pet {
public abstract void eat();
public abstract void sleep();
}
class Dog extends Pet {
Leg leftFront = new Leg(), rightFront = new Leg();
Leg leftRear = new Leg(), rightRear = new Leg();
Fur fur = new Fur();
public Fur shed() {return fur;}
public void eat() {}
public void sleep() {}
}
class Cat extends Dog {
public void ignoreOwner() {}
public void climbTree() {}
}
Which of the following statements is not a true statement?

Options.OptionText: A Cat object inherits an instance of Fur and four instances of Leg from the Dog superclass.
A Cat object is not able to sleep and eat.
A Dog object is able to climb a tree.
The relationship between Dog and Fur is an example of an appropriate use of inheritance.

m_Item1.Explanation: Relationship between Dog and Fur is an example of composition and not inheritance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which is valid declaration within an interface?
m_Item1.Explanation:
• transient is a variables modifier used in serialization.At the time of serialization, if we don’t want to save value of a particular variable in a file, then we use transient keyword.
  • When JVM comes across transient keyword, it ignores original value of the variable and save default value of that variable data type.
  • Interafce can not have protected, transient members and can not declare final method.
  • So answer 'A' is correct.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
interface A  {
int a = 1;             // 1
public int b = 2;          // 2
public static int c = 3;      // 3
public static final int d = 4;   // 4
}
Which field declaration results in a compile-time error?
m_Item1.Explanation:
By default variables in the interface are treated public, static and final even if it is not mentioned explicitly.
Example:
public interface InterfaceName
{
//Any number of final, static fields
//Any number of abstract method declarations
}

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following are Invalid statements
m_Item1.Explanation:
• As Math class is declared as final, any class can not inherit from class Math. So option A is invaid.
  • Java Math class is a part of the java.lang package.
  • Basically Math class contains methods for performing basic numeric operations such as the elementary exponential, logarithm, square root and trigonometric functions.
  • Java Math class provides several methods to work on math calculations like min(), max(), avg(), sin(),
    cos(), tan(), round(), ceil(), floor(), abs() etc
  • Java Math is a final class and it extends java.lang.Object

m_Item1.QuestionText:
interface A {int i = 1; int m1();}
interface B extends A {int i = 10; int m1();}
class C implements B {
public int m1() {
return ++i;
}
public static void main(String[] args) {
System.out.print(new C().m1());
}
}
What is the result of attempting to compile and run the program?
m_Item1.Explanation: i is a final field in the interface which can not be assigned with a new value. So program will fail for compilation at line return ++i;

m_Item1.QuestionText:
abstract class D {
String s1 = "D";
String getS1() {
    return s1;
        }
}
class E extends D {
String s1 = "E";
String getS1()
{
return s1;
}
}
class F {
public static void main (String[] s) {
D x = new E();
System.out.print(x.s1 + x.getS1());
}
}
What is the result of attempting to compile and run the program?
m_Item1.Explanation: Data binding is static in java i.e. on the basis of reference type and instance method call biding is dynamic i.e. on the basis of object type. So for x, it refers s1 from D and getS1() method gets called from class E which returns "E". So answer in "DE"

m_Item1.QuestionText:
interface A
{
void m1();        // 1
public void m2();     // 2
protected void m3();   // 3
private void m4();    // 4
abstract void m5();    // 5
}
Compile-time errors are generated at which lines?
m_Item1.Explanation: E

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Which of the following are modifiers that can be applied to an nested interface written within a interface ?
i.abstract
ii.implements
iii.final
iv.private
v.protected
vi.public
m_Item1.Explanation:
• We can declare interfaces as member of a class or another interface. Such an interface is called as member interface or nested interface.
  • This interface declared in a class can either be default, public, protected not private.
  • Interfaces (or classes) can have only public and default access specifiers when declared outside any other class.
  • Nested interface can only be declared as public or abstract.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Abstract class A 
{
private int x = 1, y = 1;
public A(int x, int y) {this.x = x; this.y = y;}
public abstract int math();
}
class B
{
static A a1 = new A(2,1) {public int math() {return x + y;}};
static A a2 = new A(2,1) {public int math() {return x - y;}};
static A a3 = new A(2,1) {public int math() {return x * y;}};
static A a4 = new A(2,1) {public int math() {return x / y;}};
public static void main(String[] args) 
{
System.out.print('' "+ a1.math() + a2.math() +a3.math() + a4.math());
}
}
What is the result of attempting to compile and run the program?
m_Item1.Explanation: E

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following combinations is valid in Java?

Options.OptionText: An abstract class implementing an interface

m_Item1.Explanation: An abstract class can implement an interface. Final abstract is invalid, and Java does not support multiple class inheritance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which type of method can't be overridden?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'final' methods cannot be overridden because it is explicitly stated they cannot be changed by subclasses.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Java, an abstract class can have:
m_Item1.Explanation: An abstract class in Java can contain both abstract methods (methods without a body) and concrete methods (methods with a body). This allows for partial abstraction.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about abstract methods in Java?

Options.OptionText: Abstract methods can have a body
Abstract methods must be declared in a non-abstract class
Abstract methods must be defined in a subclass

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract methods cannot have a body and must be implemented by subclasses of the abstract class in which they are declared.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can an abstract class be instantiated directly in Java?

Options.OptionText: Yes, if it has no abstract methods

m_Item1.Explanation: Java does not allow instantiation of abstract classes directly. They are intended to be subclasses where abstract methods are implemented.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to declare an abstract class in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'abstract' keyword is used in Java to declare a class as abstract.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How is an abstract method defined in Java?

Options.OptionText: Using the 'abstract' keyword and without a method body

m_Item1.Explanation: An abstract method in Java is declared using the 'abstract' keyword and does not include a method body.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What must a subclass do when it extends an abstract class?

Options.OptionText: It must override all methods of the abstract class
It must implement all abstract methods of the abstract class
It must implement all methods, abstract or not
It must be declared abstract as well

m_Item1.Explanation: A subclass extending an abstract class is required to implement all the abstract methods inherited from the abstract class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When does it make sense to use an abstract class in Java?

Options.OptionText: When no code is shared among different implementations
When you want to provide a base class with some default implementations
When you want all subclasses to avoid implementing methods
When you only need to use static methods

m_Item1.Explanation: An abstract class is suitable when you want a base class that provides some common implementations and forces subclasses to implement certain methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements is true about abstract classes in Java?

Options.OptionText: They can have constructors, but cannot be instantiated
They must implement all methods immediately
They can only contain static methods

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract classes in Java can have constructors, but they cannot be instantiated. Constructors are used to initialize an object of a subclass.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if a class has one or more abstract methods but is not declared as abstract?

Options.OptionText: It cannot be used for inheritance
It will cause a compile-time error
It will only allow static methods

m_Item1.Explanation: If a class contains abstract methods, it must be declared abstract. Otherwise, it generates a compile-time error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Is it possible for an abstract class to have a main method in Java?

Options.OptionText: Yes, abstract classes can have a main method
No, abstract classes cannot contain a main method
Yes, but only if it's private
Only if the class is final

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract classes in Java can contain a main method, like any other class. The main method is static and does not require an instance of the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of abstract classes in Java?

Options.OptionText: To create instances of classes directly
To provide a framework for other classes to extend and implement abstract methods

m_Item1.Explanation: Abstract classes serve as a blueprint for other classes, forcing them to implement abstract methods while allowing some shared implementation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these will properly define an abstract class in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'abstract class ClassName {}' syntax is used to define an abstract class in Java.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can a final class be declared as abstract in Java?

Options.OptionText: Yes, if all methods are implemented
No, because final classes cannot be inherited

m_Item1.Explanation: A final class cannot be inherited, and as abstract classes require inheritance for method implementation, they cannot be declared final.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can an abstract class in Java contain variables?

Options.OptionText: Yes, both static and instance variables are allowed

m_Item1.Explanation: An abstract class can contain both static and instance variables, similar to a regular class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What would be the result of trying to define a concrete method without a body in an abstract class?

Options.OptionText: The method is treated as abstract

m_Item1.Explanation: Concrete methods must have a body. Defining a method without a body and not marking it abstract will cause a compile error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following options is correct if a class inherits from an abstract class?

Options.OptionText: It must declare itself abstract if it does not implement all abstract methods
It automatically implements all abstract methods
It does not inherit any methods

m_Item1.Explanation: A class inheriting an abstract class must implement all abstract methods. If not, it must be declared abstract.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following modifiers can be used with a method in an abstract class?
m_Item1.Explanation: Methods in an abstract class can use various modifiers such as abstract, final, and synchronized, among others.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is an abstract method?
m_Item1.Explanation: An abstract method is defined without a method body, and it must be implemented by any non-abstract subclasses.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How many abstract methods must an abstract class have?
m_Item1.Explanation: An abstract class may have zero or more abstract methods. It is the presence of the abstract keyword that defines it as abstract.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Java, what happens if a class extends an abstract class and does not implement one of its abstract methods?

Options.OptionText: The class will compile without error
The class will inherit the method as abstract
The class must also be declared abstract
The class can override the method later

m_Item1.Explanation: If a class extends an abstract class and does not implement all its abstract methods, it must be declared abstract.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What keyword is used to declare an interface in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Java, the keyword 'interface' is used to declare an interface.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these statements is true about interfaces in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: Interfaces cannot have constructors as they cannot be instantiated directly.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can a class include an interface in Java?
m_Item1.Explanation: A class implements an interface using the 'implements' keyword.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When a class implements an interface, what must it do?

Options.OptionText: It must implement all methods declared in the interface.
It must declare the interface final.
It must override all method implementations from the interface.

m_Item1.Explanation: A class that implements an interface must implement all of its methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which access modifier is implicitly used for methods declared in an interface?
m_Item1.Explanation: Methods declared in an interface are implicitly public.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can an interface extend another interface in Java?

Options.OptionText: Only if the interface is abstract

m_Item1.Explanation: In Java, an interface can extend another interface using the 'extends' keyword.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the default access level for methods in an interface?
m_Item1.Explanation: All methods in an interface are by default public.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of an interface in Java?

Options.OptionText: To define methods a class must implement

m_Item1.Explanation: Interfaces in Java are used to define methods that a class must implement.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following can be defined within an interface?
m_Item1.Explanation: Since Java 8, interfaces can have static methods with implementations.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can an interface contain constants in Java?

Options.OptionText: Yes, and they are implicitly public, static, and final
Yes, but they must be final
No, constants are not allowed but variables can be

m_Item1.Explanation: In Java, any variable declared in an interface is implicitly public, static, and final.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Is it possible for a class to implement multiple interfaces?

Options.OptionText: No, a class can only implement one interface
Yes, but only if the interfaces have no methods
Yes, by separating interfaces with a comma
No, a class must extend multiple interfaces instead

m_Item1.Explanation: A class can implement multiple interfaces by separating them with a comma in the implements clause.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which feature allows an interface to have a method with an implementation?
m_Item1.Explanation: Since Java 8, interfaces can have implemented methods marked with the 'default' keyword.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword defines that a class fulfills the contract of an interface?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'implements' keyword indicates that a class will provide the concrete implementation for an interface.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Java 8, which new feature was introduced to interfaces?
m_Item1.Explanation: Java 8 introduced default methods that allow interfaces to have method implementations.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is true about interface methods implemented in classes?
m_Item1.Explanation: When implementing interface methods in a class, they must be public since the interface methods are implicitly public.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can interfaces in Java have private methods?

Options.OptionText: No, interfaces cannot have private methods
Yes, private methods are allowed since Java 8
No, but they can have protected methods

m_Item1.Explanation: From Java 9 onward, interfaces can contain private methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of these is not a characteristic of an interface?

Options.OptionText: An interface can contain abstract methods
Methods in an interface are known as abstract methods
An interface can be extended by another interface

m_Item1.Explanation: Interfaces cannot be instantiated; they can only be implemented.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When implementing an interface, what must a class provide?
m_Item1.Explanation: A class implementing an interface must provide concrete implementations for all of its methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which feature is unique to an interface compared to an abstract class?

Options.OptionText: Can contain both abstract and concrete methods

m_Item1.Explanation: Interface methods are implicitly abstract, whereas abstract classes can have non-abstract methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A class is said to honor the contract of an interface. What does this mean?

Options.OptionText: The class must be declared abstract
The class is allowed to have instance variables
The class implements all methods declared in the interface
The class must implement at least one method

m_Item1.Explanation: A class honors the contract of an interface by implementing all the methods defined in the interface.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Cloneable interface contains “clone()” method
m_Item1.Explanation: Cloneable is a marker interface having no methods.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Clone method is declared as throws
m_Item1.Explanation: clone() method throws CloneNotSupportedException if a class does not implement Cloneable interface or the fields it deals with are references of classes which does not implement Cloneable interface.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Clone() method in Object class is
m_Item1.Explanation: clone() method in Object class is declared as protected.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If u override “clone()” method u can apply access modifier
m_Item1.Explanation: Originally method declared as protected in class Object. We can widen the scope of the method which is being overridden, so protected and public scope are allowed

m_Item1.QuestionText: By default “clone” method does
m_Item1.Explanation: Clone method in class Object performs shallow copy.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Interface which does not contain any method is called as
m_Item1.Explanation: Empty interfaces are called marker interfaces.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What will happen to the following code?
interface X {
    static void disp() {
            System.out.println("in disp of X");
    }
}
public class Trial implements X {
    public static void main(String args[]) {
            Trial t=new Trial();
            t.disp();
        }
}

Options.OptionText: Compilation error “disp not available with Trial”
Compilation error “static method can not be defined inside an interface”
Compilation error “ Trial class must define disp as it is there inside parent interface”

m_Item1.Explanation: Write explaination here..

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given
interface emp // functional interface
{
String wish(String name);
}
Lambda expression in order to use above interface would be:

Options.OptionText: emp ref2=(String name)->{ return "Welcome to our site\t"+name;};
emp ref2=(String name){ return "Welcome to our site\t"+name;};

m_Item1.Explanation:
A. emp ref2 = (String name) -> { return "Welcome to our site\t" + name; };
This lambda expression takes a String name parameter and returns a formatted welcome message. The syntax (parameters) -> { body } is correct for lambda expressions.

B. emp ref2 = (String name) { return "Welcome to our site\t" + name; };
This syntax is incorrect for lambda expressions. In Java, lambda expressions require the arrow (->) notation to separate parameters and body. Simply using (parameters) { body } without the arrow is not valid for lambda expressions.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of following code ?
interface emp  {  }
public class Trial implements emp {
        public static void main(String args[])  {
                Trial t=new Trial();
                if(t instanceof Trial) {
                            System.out.println("Trial");
                }
                if(t instanceof emp)    {
                        System.out.println("emp");
          }
            if(t instanceof Object)     {
                    System.out.println("Object");
            }
        }
}

Options.OptionText: compilation error “can not use instanceof with interface”

m_Item1.Explanation: Write explaination here..

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can you declare method local variable as final and can an abstract class may be final?
m_Item1.Explanation: Local variable can be declared as final and abstract class can not be final as abstract classes need to be inherited but final classes can not be inherited.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about a final class in Java is true?

Options.OptionText: A final class can not be subclassed.
A final class cannot have any methods.
A final class can declare abstract methods.
A final class provides more flexibility for inheritance.

m_Item1.Explanation: A final class in Java is one that cannot be extended further. Once a class is declared as final, it cannot have any subclasses. This restriction is imposed to prevent inheritance and enforce the finality of the class design. A final class canot have abstract methods

m_Item1.QuestionText: String class is
m_Item1.Explanation:
The final keyword in Java prevents inheritance and subclassing, making the String class immutable and secure in its implementation.
Options B (abstract), C (static), and D (transient) are not applicable to the String class:
abstract: Abstract classes cannot be instantiated directly and must be subclassed. The String class is not abstract; it is concrete and can be instantiated.
static: Static members belong to the class rather than instances and do not apply to individual objects like String instances.
transient: Transient is used to indicate that a member variable should not be serialized, which is unrelated to the immutability and finality of the String class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Can a final method in a final class be overridden in a subclass?

Options.OptionText: Yes, it can be overridden freely.
Yes, but only if the subclass is in the same package.
Yes, but only if the method in the superclass is static.

m_Item1.Explanation: A final method in a final class cannot be overridden by any subclass. The final keyword applied to a method signifies that the method's implementation is complete and cannot be altered by subclasses, ensuring consistency and preventing unintended modifications.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Final keyword can be applied to
m_Item1.Explanation: Write explaination here..

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement about interface inheritance is TRUE?

Options.OptionText: An interface can inherit from a class
An interface can inherit from multiple interfaces
An interface cannot extend another interface
A class can inherit multiple interfaces but cannot implement them

m_Item1.Explanation: One interface can extend from multiple interfaces
